blocks.html:
Keoni

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45562203

Cell-free biology

take the machinery of life out of the cell and run it as a chemical system

You lyse cells (usually E. coli, wheat germ, or rabbit reticulocytes), keep the transcription–translation machinery (ribosomes, tRNAs, enzymes, energy regeneration), and discard the rest. What remains is essentially a programmable biochemical computer

You could:

  • Rapidly prototype enzymes for carbon fixation or cellulose synthesis.
  • Test synthetic pathways without waiting on plant regeneration.

What makes cells, cells?

  • Compartmentalisation
    • maintains non-equilibrium concentration gradients
    • selectively imports/exports substances
    • localises reactions spatially
  • Energy closure
    • internally regenerates its own energy source (ATP, proton motive force) from environmental inputs
  • Self-maintenance (homeostasis)
    • Repairs damaged components
    • Regulates pH, iron balance, redox
    • Stabilises reaction rates
  • Reproductive capability
    • Internally stores genetic code
    • Can replicate itself

hypothetically how can you build a replicating material without needing a cell-like construct from chem alone

right cool. so what is wood? as a chemical substance.

okay. what cna we delete from wood's definition so it's still useful for construction?

Wood is a carbon-based fiber–matrix composite whose mesoscale anisotropy produces high specific stiffness and a characteristic grain texture.

  • load-bearing phase: aligned stiff polymer fibers (cellulose-like) carrying tensile stress
  • matrix phase: cross-linked polymer binding fibers, providing compression and shear transfer
  • texture source: spatial variation in fiber orientation and density at millimeter scales

in 1978 Genentech developed synthetic humanized insulin by joining its gene with a plasmid vector inserted into the bacterium Escherichia coli. Insulin, widely used for the treatment of diabetes, was previously extracted from the pancreas of abattoir animals (cattle or pigs). The genetically engineered bacteria are able to produce large quantities of synthetic human insulin at relatively low cost

Expression in Escherichia coli of chemically synthesized genes for human insulin. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC382885/

the chloroplast that allows plants to harness the energy of the Sun to make food originated when eukaryotes swallowed a type of photosynthesizing bacteria known as cyanobacteria, or blue-green algae. The symbiosis that formed between the two organisms, in which the eukaryote provided protection while the cyanobacteria provided food, is what formed the basis for the evolution of the hundreds of thousands of plants we see today

The origin of the chloroplast

https://telegra.ph/PlantLab-10-09

Towards synthetic biological approaches to resource utilization on space missions https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4277073/

How To Grow (Almost) Anything 2026 Submission Deadline: January 16, 2026 at 11:59pm PT https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSdoBI_sJXZSfHs7k19Xu-XUWnF4I2J0Gmvdhqd01e-Y5Ruorg/viewform

This is a cheap and easy way of mass-producing the protein, for example, utilizing the rapid reproduction of E.coli with a plasmid containing the insulin gene leads to a large production of insulin

https://howtogrowalmostanything.notion.site/Week-4-Protein-Design-Part-I-126387c9c1e980279764e603eb13c832

https://howtogrowalmostanything.notion.site/Week-6-Genetic-Circuits-Part-I-143387c9c1e980f0bc7ac8fe29fd92a8

https://www.thenakedscientists.com/podcasts/question-week/could-green-humans-harvest-energy-sun

https://drive.google.com/file/d/1S0Ihri2GZkukezMpaVit3hEJ6qWjoAt4/view

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46221594

I wonder if you could use this to generate the fractal terrain by the ocean like in Sydney. We already have "artificial reefs". We have built beauty in the build environment (architecture), some in the natural environment (gardens, forests), but comparatively less when it comes to the ocean.

https://x.com/owl_posting/status/2006423043220873531/photo/1

Events:

  • iGEM conference - Paris - 13 Nov

Questions:

  • How do I make money from fooling around with plants?
  • 26K Axiom SNP array (Affymetrix) - why only 26k? is this enough for whole genome of aplant?
  • How did they estimate the age of a gene?
    • estimated the age of tga1-maize using a genealogy-based method, finding that the allele arose approximately 42,000 to 49,000 y ago, predating the beginning of maize domestication (from https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2503748122)

Insights:

  • Plant reference genomes are on an exponential curve up since 2000 (from 0 plants in early 2000 to now 1000 plant genomes).

Observations:

  • There are so few genomes out there.
  • All of the genomic databases are so shitty.
  • The tools used to scan plant genomes do so at a fidelity which is useful - they don’t capture the entire genome.
  • The tools used to scan genomes would be very high leverage tech to improve.
  • My instincts were correct- automated plant breeding and cycle length is an important new thing!

Ideas:

  • It looks like people still manually:
    • inspect crops
    • go out to sites and track disease
    • etc.
  • UAV phenotyping
    • Predicting below-ground traits using UAV
    • https://academic.oup.com/jxb/article/76/17/5161/8177095
  • Codesigning biology to match robot pickers
    • https://x.com/QAAFI/status/1970971838743240906

Works:

  • Rose engineered https://x.com/NickDesnoyer/status/1867165034633110008
  • multi-crop speed breeding protocols in @NaturePlants
  • Essentials of Plant Breeding (book) and Breeding for Quantitative Traits in Plants
  • “Principles of Development”, “the molecular life of plants” or “Arabidopsis: an atlas of morphology and development”.
  • Plants + bio-engineered bacteria that break down VO2 (paris)

Communities:

  • IGEM - https://technology.igem.org/
  • https://petri.bio/#companies
  • https://founderledbio.com/tour/san-francisco/
  • https://founderledbio.com/tour/new-york-city/
  • https://founderledbio.com/tour/london/
  • Australian Farm Institute - https://www.farminstitute.org.au/faster-computers-breeding-better-plants/

People:

  • https://x.com/HickeyLab

Topics:

  • Biotech
    • DNA sequencing
    • Cryo-EM
    • X-ray crystallography
    • Synbio
    • Flow cytometry
    • Stable and radioactive isotopes
    • Proteomics
    • Lipidomics & Metabolomics
    • Mass Spectrometry
  • Genotyping
    • 26K SNP chip
    • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SNP_genotyping
    • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SNP_array
    • ~20–30K evenly spaced SNPs can capture most common haplotypes for GS/GWAS scanning; if LD decays fast or the genome is huge/polyploid (e.g., wheat), you’ll want more density or sequencing
  • Phenotyping at scale using UAV
    • Capture traits that are correlated/can be used to predict traits of interest to plant breeders
    • UAV’s can capture: wavelengths
    • https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rzIkDwFrctQ
  • Utilising LLM’s for plant breeding
    • https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1583344/full
  • Engineering plants to match robots
    • Engineering crop flower morphology facilitates robotization of cross-pollination and speed breeding
      • https://www.cell.com/cell/abstract/S0092-8674(25)00840-2
    • https://www.nature.com/articles/s41477-025-02117-3
  • Speed Breeding
    • https://www.nature.com/articles/s41477-017-0083-8
    • How it works.
      • Photoperiod extension: For example a light-dark regime of about 22 hours light / 2 hours dark, vs typical ~12‐14 h light in glasshouse/field. This accelerates flowering. jart.co.in+2Nature+2
      • Controlled temperature and environment: Day/night temperatures and humidity are optimised to speed plant growth and development. jart.co.in
      • High light intensity / optimal spectrum: Ensures robust photosynthesis and growth under extended light. petiolepro.com+1
      • Reduced generation length: By harvesting seeds earlier (sometimes immature seeds), rapid transition to next generation. jart.co.in+1
      • High planting density (in some cases) and efficient use of space/environment to push through many plants/generations. jart.co.in
  • Computational Plant Breeding

A figure showcasing the release of plant reference genome data in the NCBI database ranging from 2000 to 2024

LOVE 4 engineering.

Useful properties

How can we grow plants faster?

Sharks and stuff.

Hearts and fields.

Plants and hardware.

Morphology model.

Evolution, NN’s.

Ideas.

resonant sensors

Biolab plan.

Course ideas.

bioelectricity

Diabetes.

Fundraise ideas.

HTGAA.

Questions:

  • How do I make money from fooling around with plants?
  • 26K Axiom SNP array (Affymetrix) - why only 26k? is this enough for whole genome of aplant?
  • How did they estimate the age of a gene?
    • estimated the age of tga1-maize using a genealogy-based method, finding that the allele arose approximately 42,000 to 49,000 y ago, predating the beginning of maize domestication (from https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2503748122)

Insights:

  • Plant reference genomes are on an exponential curve up since 2000 (from 0 plants in early 2000 to now 1000 plant genomes).

Observations:

  • There are so few genomes out there.
  • All of the genomic databases are so shitty.
  • The tools used to scan plant genomes do so at a fidelity which is useful - they don’t capture the entire genome.
  • The tools used to scan genomes would be very high leverage tech to improve.
  • My instincts were correct- automated plant breeding and cycle length is an important new thing!

  • Genotyping
    • 26K SNP chip
    • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SNP_genotyping
    • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SNP_array
    • ~20–30K evenly spaced SNPs can capture most common haplotypes for GS/GWAS scanning; if LD decays fast or the genome is huge/polyploid (e.g., wheat), you’ll want more density or sequencing
  • Phenotyping at scale using UAV
    • Capture traits that are correlated/can be used to predict traits of interest to plant breeders
    • UAV’s can capture: wavelengths
    • https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rzIkDwFrctQ
  • Utilising LLM’s for plant breeding
    • https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1583344/full
  • Engineering plants to match robots
    • Engineering crop flower morphology facilitates robotization of cross-pollination and speed breeding
      • https://www.cell.com/cell/abstract/S0092-8674(25)00840-2
    • https://www.nature.com/articles/s41477-025-02117-3
  • Speed Breeding
    • https://www.nature.com/articles/s41477-017-0083-8
    • How it works.
      • Photoperiod extension: For example a light-dark regime of about 22 hours light / 2 hours dark, vs typical ~12‐14 h light in glasshouse/field. This accelerates flowering. jart.co.in+2Nature+2
      • Controlled temperature and environment: Day/night temperatures and humidity are optimised to speed plant growth and development. jart.co.in
      • High light intensity / optimal spectrum: Ensures robust photosynthesis and growth under extended light. petiolepro.com+1
      • Reduced generation length: By harvesting seeds earlier (sometimes immature seeds), rapid transition to next generation. jart.co.in+1
      • High planting density (in some cases) and efficient use of space/environment to push through many plants/generations. jart.co.in
  • Computational Plant Breeding
